design_philosophy.core_loop: Acquire properties, collect rent, bankrupt opponents through resource management and negotiation
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Economic competition and risk assessment—deciding when to invest in properties versus maintaining cash reserves
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Build a real estate empire and accumulate wealth through strategic purchasing and smart negotiations
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Faithful adaptation of board game rules to digital format; emphasizes turn-based strategic decision-making over action; relies on house rules and player-negotiated gameplay rather than systemic enforcement

difficulty.continues_system: Game continues until all opponents are bankrupt; no hard fail state
difficulty.checkpointing: Game state persists across turns; no checkpoint system needed
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Player controls game length, rule variations, and opponent difficulty through initial setup; can choose number and skill level of AI opponents

progression.upgrade_system: Property acquisition and house/hotel construction; players progressively build monopolies and increase rental income
progression.unlock_mechanism: Properties unlock houses/hotels once player owns full color set; progression is emergent from board state rather than predetermined
replayability_hooks: Negotiable house rules affect gameplay flow
Random property distribution changes strategy each game

world_building_techniques: Property names establish real-world locations (streets, utilities, railroads)
Themed board editions (different city settings) provide geographic flavor

faction_indicators: Property ownership colors create visual faction territories
danger_signals: Opponent cash reserves indicate threat level
Concentrated property monopolies signal economic dominance
Utility and railroad ownership by single player shows resource control
environmental_storytelling: Board layout shows developed vs. undeveloped regions
Property colors and ownership distribution reveal game state hierarchy
Housing density on board indicates economic disparity
